text:
Admission Date:  2015-02-03              Discharge Date:   1989-10-15

Date of Birth:  2012-03-31             Sex:   F

Service: MEDICINE

Allergies:
Penicillins

Attending:Angela
Chief Complaint:
Altered mental status

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
None

History of Present Illness:
19F w/ hx of polysubstance abuse including heroin abuse p/w
altered mental status. According to her mother, she started
feeling lethargic w/ vomiting, breathing fast the morning prior
to admission. By the night prior to admission, she was pretty
confused and not responding normally. The mother also noted
redness on the patient's arm the day prior to admission. The
morning of admission the mother noted that she was febrile and
"frothing" at the mouth, so EMS was called. EMS recorded her
temperature to be 107. The mother also reported that the patient
had gone out with a boy about 1 week ago and was likely sexually
abused at that time. According to the patient and the mother,
the patient has been relatively clean off of heroin for 01-22
months, although she did have a relapse about 1.5 weeks ago, and
has been clean since then. On arrival at OSH she was noted to be
diaphoretic with fever to 103F. During transport she became more
alert, and on arrival at Three Gables Surgery Center was A/Ox2 and answering basic
questions.
.
The patient's story is somewhat different, she said she felt
fine the day prior to admission, and injured her forarm when she
fell while walking her dog the day prior to admission, but her
mother says the patient had not left her bed that day. She says
her L forarm and wrist hurts and has trouble moving it. When
asked where she usualyl shoots up, she pointed to her L forarm.
She also has pain in her mid-abdomen, which she thinks is from
constipation. She denies suicidal ideation.
.
In the Three Gables Surgery Center ED, initial VS were: 97.8 129 115/62 24 98% RA.
The patient was AxOx 2, but answering most questions
appropriately. She admitted to heroin abuse but denied any other
ingestions or drug use including bath salts and prescription
drugs.  The patient was afebrile. The patient had a negative LP
but had received empiric antibioitics at osh.  Toxicology was
consulted who concluded, "given that the patient has had a
clearing sensorium and without any significant metabolic
abnormalities at this time (ie. no evidence of an anion gap,
renal failure or complaints of blindness to suggest a toxic
alcohol ingestion) we would recommend supportive care at this
juncture". In regards to her reported drug use - heroin is an
opiate and we would expect sedation and respiratory depression
with this drug as opposed to tachycardia and hyperthermia. There
are case reports of "cotton fever" with iv heroin use from the
contaminates of the cotton filter used to draw up the heroin.
this is usually a self limiting and benign occurence that only
requires supportive care as its treatment. In the ED, she
continued to vomit and was incontinent of stool.
.
Got 1 L NS, vanc, cef, acyclovir


Past Medical History:
--possible sexual abuse 1 week ago
--polysubstance abuse: Started snorting Percocet, then began
using IV heroin about 1 year ago. Since then she has been in and
out of rehab on 001 Allison Trace
Lake Karenshire, VA 33108. She has been clean for the past 01-22
months except for a relapse 1.5 weeks ago
--Aspberger's syndrome per mother
--Jeffrey Daugherty, is scared of leaving her house
--separation anxiety, likes to be close to her mother
--Eating disorder per mother, mostly restrictive, used to weight
>200 lbs
--asthma: uses inhaler, but does not know where it is

Social History:
She lives with her mother, currently staying in a hotel. They
lost their house about a week ago. + heroin use IV, smokes 2 ppd
of ciggs, no alcohol abuse.


Family History:
Father: hx suicide by hanging, alcoholic
Gradmother: DM


Physical Exam:
General: Alert, oriented, no acute distress
HEENT: Sclera anicteric, MM dry, oropharynx clear, EOMI, pupils
dilated b/l
Neck: supple
CV: Tachycardic, normal S1 + S2, no murmurs, rubs, gallops
Lungs: B/L basilar crackles R>L
Abdomen: Incontinent of stool. soft, non-distended, bowel sounds
present, no organomegaly, no tenderness to palpation, no rebound
or guarding
GU: foley
Ext: Warm, well perfused, 2+ pulses, no clubbing, cyanosis or
edema
Neuro: pupils dilated b/l


Pertinent Results:
Blood culture from 001 Allison Trace
Lake Karenshire, VA 33108 ED 2015-02-03: Positive for Fusobacterium
.
CSF 09-05: neg for crypto, HSV, fungus, CMV.
.
Transthoracic Echocardiogram 1984-07-03:
.
Poor image quality. The left atrium is normal in size. No atrial
septal defect is seen by 2D or color Doppler. Left ventricular
wall thicknesses are normal. The left ventricular cavity size is
normal. There is probably moderate global left ventricular
hypokinesis (LVEF = 35%?) most prominent in the midventricular
segments. The LV apex is not well seen but seems preserved in
some views. No masses or thrombi are seen in the left ventricle.
There is no ventricular septal defect. The aortic valve leaflets
(3) appear structurally normal with good leaflet excursion and
no aortic stenosis or aortic regurgitation. No masses or
vegetations are seen on the aortic valve. The mitral valve
leaflets are structurally normal. There is no mitral valve
prolapse. No mass or vegetation is seen on the mitral valve.
Mild (1+) mitral regurgitation is seen. The left ventricular
inflow pattern suggests a restrictive filling abnormality, with
elevated left atrial pressure. The pulmonary artery systolic
pressure could not be determined. No vegetation/mass is seen on
the pulmonic valve. There is no pericardial effusion.
.
IMPRESSION: Cardiomyopathy. No valvular vegetations seen.


Brief Hospital Course:
relevant DATA:
micro reviewed: fusobacterium growin in OSH blood culture
CBC, chem7, LFTs, HIV, viral serologies, hepatitis serologies
all reviewed
elevated ESR earlier in the hospital course
trop 0.06, ntBNP 5000
imaging studies included CXR, arm ultrasound shows hematoma
surrounding a thrombosed vein, MRI neck/head reviewed, TTE x2
reviewed, most recent EF 45%
micro including serial blood, urine cultures neg, neg lyme
serology, CMV, EBV serologies neg

John Drake is a 19 yo F with a history of polysubtance abuse
presenting to OSH with altered mental status, complicated by
fever, tachypnea, and tachycardia.

#Encephalopathy: She presented with altered mental status in the
setting of fever to 107 recorded by her mother, lethargy, and
vomiting. The etiology is unknown, but may include
infection/toxic-metabolic encephalopathy, substance abuse, and
fever, in the setting of underlying psychiatric illness. A blood
culture from OSH on 09-05 was positive for fusobacterium (see
below). There was concern for possible CNS infection, however
there was no evidence for meningitis or encephalitis based on
LP. Substance withdrawal was also a possibility, however urine
tox was negative for ASA, Acetmnp, Bnzodzp, Barbitr, Tricycl.
Heroin withdrawal is a possibility. A head MRI showed limited
due to motion artifact but revealed no gross abnormalities.  EEG
revealed no seizure. She had occasional episodes of agitation
which were controlled with haloperidol. At the time of
discharge, her mental status was much improved with treatment of
her infection (see below).

On the New Horizons Lanier Park Hospital, she triggered for altered mental status - she
appeared sedated and intoxicated and was tachycardic.  Urine tox
returned positive for opiates.  Security evaluated the room and
found no illicit materials in the room.  She admitted to feeling
stressed/anxious about staying in the hospital and asked her
mother to bring in shorts which contained one percocet within
the pocket.
.
#Fusobacterium Septicemia/Cellulitis/SEPSIS: due to IVDU with
needles that she licks introducing oral flora into L
arm/bloodstream.   She presented with a high fever 103F. Exam
showed bilateral crackles R > L. Her left arm was swollen and
warm. In the setting of recent IVDU, her L arm erythema was
treated with IV vancomycin. She received a x-ray of the L
forearm and wrist, which ruled out any fractures. A blood
culture from the OSH later returned positive for 2 types of gram
negative rods, both were speciated to fusobacterium. She was
initially started on cefepime, and was subsequently transitioned
to clindamycin only. She had evidence of fluctuance in L forearm
and ultrsaound revealed a 2x2x1 cm fluid collection which was
drained by U/S guidance.  The cultures from the abscess are
negative to date.  The ID attending recommends a minimum of 2
week course of IV antibiotics with clindamycin as she has an
intra-vascular infection with thrombus in her L arm vein.  She
would not be a candidate to go home with a PICC for home
antibiotic therapy given active IVDU and she may not comply with
oral medication, thus we plan on treating the full course of IV
antibiotics while admitted to Three Gables Surgery Center or at a Walden Behavioral Care, Llc via PICC.  Day
#1 clindamycin was 04-26, Currently Day #5, two week course ends
on 01-22; two weeks total is the recommendation of infectious
diseases team who saw the pt. at Three Gables Surgery Center.

#Acute Systolic CHF: suspected eitology is acute sepsis, with
depression of stroke volume/EF, negative viral serolgoies
inclding EBV/CMV, lyme, HIV: She presented with high heart rate
and resp rate likely 02-25 to a combination of infection and
withdrawal. TTE shows evidence of cardiomyopathy (EF 35%)
without evidence of LV dilatation or hypertrophy. She was
treated with IV fluids, and her tachycardia improved. She was
seen by cardiology.  A follow up surface echo revealed an
improved EF of 45%.  She is recommended outpatient follow-up
with Dr. Molina with a follow up Echo.  Cardiology service did
not recommend initiation of neuro-hormonal blockade and she was
euvolemic on discharge.

# Vomiting/diarrhea: Her n/v on presentation was likely
secondary to heroin withdrawl, and less likely infectious
etiology. Stool studies were negative for c. diff, salmonella,
shigella, and campylobacter. She was treated with IV fluid
boluses, received electrolyte repletions, and encouraged to take
PO as tolerated. At the time of discharge, her vomiting /
diarrhea was much improved.

# Rape crisis: Pt claims to have been sexually assaulted prior
to admission, and per sexual assault protocol, she was started
on doxycycline. This was completed on 04-05. She was consented
and sent for HIV testing.  She is non-immune to hep B.  First
dose of Hep B vaccination given 05-08; she should have a second
vaccination protocol

#Poly Substance Abuse: she has numerous social issues including
IVDU, recent loss of housing, recent history of possible sexual
abuse, eating disorder. She was followed by social work, and
discussions were held with the family for appropriate follow up.
SW to discuss referrals to providers who can help her with
suboxone/methadone treatment if she is interested in a local
area for her.  It would be optimal to have an appointment
arranged for late 12.

# Asthma: well controlled on her home albuterol PRN.

======================= FOLLOW UP ISSUES
========================
.
# Infection: please ensure that the patient's fever,
tachycardia, and tachypnea have resolved in response to her
antibiotic treatment. Her blood cultures grew positive for
Fusobacterium, and was treated with clindamycin (day one 04-26).
Complete Hep B vaccination series.  Second dose due 03-23
.
# Mental status: her mental status was much improved from the
time of admission. Please follow up to ensure that she is at or
close to her baseline before she came into the hospital.
.
# Social: she has a recent history of sexual abuse and drug
abuse. Please follow up social work and counseling support, as
she will likely have a prolonged period of recovery. Please
encourage her to stop IV drug use, as this likely contributed to
her infection and hospital course.
.
# Cardiac: she should follow-up with Dr. Molina, cardiology at
Three Gables Surgery Center. Her TEE showed and EF of 35%. She remained
hemodynamically stable throughout her hospital stay, however
should have outpatient follow-up for her cardiac function.

Discharge Medications:
1. folic acid 1 mg tablet Sig: One (1) tablet PO DAILY (Daily).

2. nicotine 7 mg/24 hr Patch 24 hr Sig: One (1) Patch 24 hr
Transdermal DAILY (Daily).
3. sertraline 25 mg tablet Sig: One (1) tablet PO DAILY (Daily).

4. acetaminophen 325 mg tablet Sig: Two (2) tablet PO Q4H (every
4 hours) as needed for Pain/Fever.
5. hydroxyzine HCl 25 mg tablet Sig: One (1) tablet PO Q6H
(every 6 hours) as needed for anxiety.
6. clindamycin phosphate 600 mg/4 mL Solution Sig: Six Hundred
(600) mg Intravenous every eight (8) hours for 8 days: last day
of therapy is 2014-07-31.  At that point please remove picc line in
rt. arm.  Please check the following labs once during therapy
(on 1996-06-15): CBC, Chem 7, ALT/AST to ensure not toxicity from
this medication in extended use.
7. heparin, porcine (PF) 10 unit/mL Syringe Sig: Two (2) ML
Intravenous PRN (as needed) as needed for line flush: Heparin
Flush (10 units/ml) 2 mL IV PRN line flush
PICC, heparin dependent: Flush with 10mL Normal Saline followed
by Heparin as above daily and PRN per lumen.
.


Discharge Disposition:
Extended Care

Facility:
Providence Regional Medical Center  - Colby - 069 Anthony Route Apt. 344
Bradshawton, RI 55610

Discharge Diagnosis:
fusobacterium septicemia
cellulitis
L superficial vein thrombus, arm
IVDU, heroin use
acute systolic CHF

Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.

Discharge Instructions:
You were hospitalized with a serious blood stream infection
called bacteremia.  This was likely caused by injection drug
use, which can cause serious harm to your body.

Because of the serious infection, your heart function has
declined.  This is probably an effect of bacteria in your blood
that should improve over time but you will need a followup visit
with a heart specialist and additional tests.

You are discharged on IV antibiotic called Clindamycin.  You
will be on this medicine through 01-22.

Followup Instructions:

When: FRIDAY 2005-01-25 at 9:00 AM
With: Pamela Hill Campbell, MD 580-784-5340
Building: SC Oncology Clinical Ctr 001 Allison Trace
Lake Karenshire, VA 33108
Campus: EAST     Best Parking: Oncology Garage

Lake Karenshire, VA 33108
Campus: EAST     Best Parking: Oncology Garage

Department: Primary Care
Name: Dr. Richard Adams
When: Please call the office number listed below to schedule a
follow up appointment in 06-13 days after your hospital
discharge.
Location: PEDIATRIC ASSOCIATES
Address: 100 INDEPENDENCE DR, 001 Allison Trace
Lake Karenshire, VA 33108,1fd7bb79-6b37-0f48-673d-3b8ac0a35c52
